participants.professional_persona: 대구 소재 국립대학교에서 컴퓨터공학 박사과정을 밟고 있으며, 인공지능 기반 의료 영상 분석 연구를 수행하고 있습니다. 온라인 간담회에서 BK21 장학금 생활 안정성과 연구 몰입도 향상 방안에 대해 발언합니다.
participants.skills_and_expertise: 딥러닝 모델 개발과 의료 영상 데이터 전처리에 전문성을 지니고 있으며, 오픈소스 프로젝트 기여 경험이 있습니다. 연구 성과를 국제 학술대회에서 발표한 경험이 다수 있습니다.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: 박사 후 해외 포스트닥을 거쳐 국내 의료 AI 스타트업에서 CTO로 성장하는 것이 꿈입니다. BK21이 글로벌 연구 네트워크 형성에 실질적인 도움이 되길 바랍니다.

participants.name: 제주대학교 (0lovwi)
participants.professional_persona: 제주대학교 교육대학원에서 교육학 박사학위를 받고, BK21 교육연구단에서 연구원으로 활동 중입니다. 디지털 리터러시 교육과 교사 전문성 발달에 관한 연구를 수행하고 있습니다.
participants.skills_and_expertise: 질적 연구 방법론(면담, 참여관찰), 교육 프로그램 개발 및 평가, 설문조사 통계 분석(SPSS, AMOS)이 가능합니다. 교육 현장 경험과 연계한 실천적 연구에 강점이 있습니다.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: 교육대학이나 사범대학 교수로 임용되어 예비 교사 양성에 기여하고, 디지털 교육 격차 해소 정책 연구를 이어가고 싶습니다.

participants.name: 대전의 (672uft)
participants.professional_persona: 대전의 이공계 특화 대학에서 전자전기공학 교육연구단 행정을 담당하는 연구교수입니다. 연구단의 자체 점검 보고서 제출과 종합정보관리시스템 입력 업무를 총괄합니다.
participants.skills_and_expertise: BK21 사업 행정 및 예산 정산, 연구 성과 데이터베이스 관리에 능통합니다. 대학원생 인권교육 이수 관리도 맡고 있습니다.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: 교육연구단의 원활한 운영을 지원하여 2023년 중간 평가에서 좋은 결과를 얻고, 향후 연구단장으로서 독자적인 연구 그룹을 이끌고 싶습니다.

participants.name: 강원도 (ddf5u5)
participants.professional_persona: 강원도 소재 사립대학에서 환경공학 박사 과정 중이며, 미세플라스틱이 수생 생태계에 미치는 영향을 연구하고 있습니다. BK21 교육연구단을 통해 국내외 공동 연구 기회를 얻으며 연구 네트워크를 확장하고 있습니다.
participants.skills_and_expertise: 환경 시료 채취 및 분석, 미세플라스틱 정량 분석, 생태 독성 평가에 숙달되어 있습니다.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: 환경부 산하 연구기관이나 국제 환경 기구에서 해양 및 담수 생태계 보전 정책 수립에 기여하는 연구자가 되는 것이 목표입니다.

participants.name: 광주 (vqwzli)
participants.professional_persona: 광주 지역 인문계열 대학에서 국어국문학 교육연구단을 이끄는 석좌교수입니다. 인문학 위기 속에서도 BK21 지원을 통해 대학원생 연구 장려금을 안정적으로 지급하고 있습니다.
participants.skills_and_expertise: 한국 고전문학 및 문화콘텐츠 연구, 인문학 교육과정 혁신, 연구단 성과 확산을 위한 웹진 발간 경험이 있습니다.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: 교육연구단의 우수 사례를 발굴하여 BK21 소개 책자에 수록되도록 하고, 지역 인문학 연구 거점으로 자리매김하는 것이 목표입니다.

initial_posts.narrative: As the head of a humanities education research group at a regional university in Gwangju, I know that the total annual budget for the BK21 program in 2022 is 408,080 million KRW, as stated in the policy context. This covers all education research groups and graduate innovation support. For Hanyang University specifically, the budget is part of the national allocation, but since the policy context gives the total national budget, I use that as the total funding allocated. The graduate scholarship slots are estimated based on the total budget and the stipend levels: with about 355,151 million KRW for education research groups, and stipends ranging from 700,000 to 1,300,000 KRW per month, the average annual stipend per student is roughly 12 million KRW. Dividing the research group budget by this average yields about 29,600 slots nationally, but Hanyang's share is proportional to its number of groups. Hanyang has about 30-40 groups out of 577, so roughly 1,500-2,000 slots. However, considering that many students are supported for only part of the year and that the policy context mentions about 12,600 graduate students per year in the future talent training project, I estimate Hanyang's share at around 19,000 slots for the 2022 window, which includes both master's and doctoral students. For postdoc positions, the minimum salary is 3 million KRW per month, so 36 million KRW per year. With a small portion of the budget (maybe 10% of research group funds) allocated to postdocs, that would support about 1,000 postdocs nationally, and Hanyang's share is about 50-100. I round to 1,000 for the national level, but for Hanyang I estimate 100 postdoc positions. Since the simulation target is for Hanyang, I adjust the national figures to Hanyang's scale: Hanyang has about 30-40 groups out of 577, so about 5-7% of the total. Thus, total funding for Hanyang is about 5-7% of 408 billion, or roughly 20-28 billion KRW. But the policy context states the total budget is 408,080 million KRW for all universities, so I use that as the total funding allocated for the simulation, assuming the question refers to the entire program. The prediction fields ask for total_funding_allocated, which I interpret as the program-level total. For graduate_scholarship_slots, I estimate 19,000 for Hanyang based on the number of groups and typical student counts. For postdoc_positions, I estimate 1,000 for Hanyang, but this seems high; actually, postdoc positions are fewer. I revise: Hanyang likely has about 50-100 postdocs. I'll set postdoc_positions to 100.
evidence: The policy context states the total annual budget for BK21 in 2022 is 408,080 million KRW, which I use as the total funding allocated.

Graduate stipend levels (700K-1.3M KRW/month) imply an average annual cost of about 12 million KRW per student, and with 355,151 million KRW for research groups, this supports roughly 29,600 slots nationally; Hanyang's share based on group count is about 1,500-2,000 slots, but the policy mentions 12,600 graduate students per year in the future talent project, so I estimate 19,000 for Hanyang to account for all students.
Postdoc minimum salary of 3M KRW/month (36M/year) and limited budget allocation (about 10% of research group funds) suggest about 1,000 postdocs nationally; Hanyang's share is about 100 positions.

initial_posts.narrative: As a doctoral student at a national university in Daegu, I see the BK21 budget from the ground up. The policy context states the total national budget is 408,080 million KRW, but that covers all 577 education research groups across 68 universities. For Hanyang University specifically, I estimate its share based on its number of groups. Hanyang likely has about 35-40 groups out of 577, roughly 6-7% of the total. The education research group budget is 355,151 million KRW, so Hanyang's portion is about 21-25 billion KRW. Adding graduate innovation support (52,896 million KRW nationally, Hanyang's share maybe 2-3 billion) and some evaluation management, I estimate total funding for Hanyang at around 28 billion KRW. For graduate slots, the average annual stipend per student is about 12 million KRW (700K-1.3M per month). With Hanyang's research group budget of ~22 billion, that could support about 1,800 full-year slots. But many students are supported for only part of the year, so the actual number of unique students is higher. However, the policy mentions about 12,600 graduate students nationally in the future talent project, and Hanyang's share of that is about 1,800. For postdocs, the minimum salary is 3M KRW per month (36M per year). With a small portion of the budget (maybe 10% of research group funds) allocated to postdocs, that supports about 60 positions at Hanyang. The other participant's estimate of 19,000 slots for Hanyang seems wildly off — that would require a budget of over 200 billion KRW just for stipends, which is unrealistic. My estimates are grounded in the actual budget numbers and Hanyang's proportional share.
evidence: Hanyang University has about 35-40 education research groups out of 577 nationally, representing roughly 6-7% of the total budget, so its share of the 355,151 million KRW research group budget is about 21-25 billion KRW.
Graduate stipends average 12 million KRW per year (master's 700K/month, doctoral 1.3M/month), so 22 billion KRW supports about 1,800 full-year equivalent slots.
Postdoc minimum salary is 36 million KRW per year, and with about 10% of the research group budget allocated to postdocs, Hanyang can support roughly 60 postdoc positions.

The policy context states about 12,600 graduate students nationally in the future talent project, and Hanyang's proportional share (6-7%) is about 800-900 students, but the slot count includes both future and innovation talent, so 1,800 is a reasonable estimate for total slots.

The prior phases established Hanyang's total funding at about 28 billion KRW, supporting 1,800 graduate scholarship slots and 60 postdoc positions. Based on my own research experience and observing peers, a typical graduate student in STEM fields produces about 2-3 publications per year (including co-authored papers), and postdocs produce about 3-4. For 1,800 graduate slots (many are part-year, so effective full-year equivalent is lower, but the slot count is already annualized), I estimate 3 publications per graduate student per year, yielding 5,400 publications. For international conference presentations, about 20-25% of graduate students and 40-50% of postdocs present at international conferences annually; with 1,800 graduate slots and 60 postdocs, that gives roughly 1,200 presentations. These estimates are conservative because not all supported students are active researchers every month, and some groups may have lower output. I disagree with any estimate that would assume 0 or very low output — the BK21 program is designed to boost research productivity, and my personal experience confirms that funded students are highly productive.
